did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a custom seven-layered deep convolutional neural network (CNN) trained from scratch, as well as transfer learning with modified AlexNet and ResNet-18 models, to classify acral melanoma and benign nevi using dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work, including the development of a custom seven-layered deep convolutional neural network and the application of transfer learning with AlexNet and ResNet-18. It reports new results from training and testing on a specific dataset of 724 dermoscopic images, achieving specific accuracy metrics (e.g., >90% for the custom model, ~97% for transfer learning), which characterizes it as a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Acral melanoma detection using dermoscopic images and convolutional neural networks
journal: Visual Computing for Industry, Biomedicine, and Art
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of detecting acral melanoma (AM), a rare and lethal skin cancer subtype, using dermoscopic images. The researchers utilized a dataset of 724 histopathologically confirmed images from the Yonsei University Health System, comprising 350 AM and 374 benign nevus cases. They developed a custom seven-layered deep convolutional neural network trained from scratch and compared its performance against transfer learning models using modified AlexNet and ResNet-18. The custom model achieved an accuracy of over 90% for classifying AM and benign nevi, while the transfer learning approach yielded an average accuracy of nearly 97%. The results indicate that the proposed deep learning systems can effectively classify skin cancer and assist dermatologists in early clinical decision-making.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer subtype; 350 histopathologically confirmed cases in the dataset.
Benign skin lesion; 374 histopathologically confirmed cases in the dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into Acral Melanoma (AM) and Benign Nevus (BN) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification of melanoma subtypes' and 'classifying skin cancer'. The models (custom 7-layered CNN, modified AlexNet, modified ResNet-18) predict discrete diagnostic categories (AM vs. BN). Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-Score, and AROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve), which are standard for classification tasks. The dataset consists of 350 AM and 374 benign nevus cases.

datasets_used.name: Yonsei University Health System Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 724 histopathologically confirmed dermoscopic images of skin lesions acquired from Severance Hospital in the Yonsei University Health System, Seoul, South Korea. The dataset is publicly available via figshare.

use.training: Used to train the custom seven-layered deep convolutional neural network (CNN) and the modified AlexNet and ResNet-18 models (70% of images).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (20% of images).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the algorithm on unseen images (10% of images).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: AROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the average accuracy and its variability for the custom ConvNet, modified ResNet-18, and modified AlexNet models.
To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the three deep learning models (custom ConvNet, ResNet-18, AlexNet) by plotting True Positive Rate against False Positive Rate.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation of accuracy) and ROC curve analysis.

Yonsei University Health System Dataset.notes: The modified ResNet-18 model achieved the best average accuracy. The ROC curve value for ResNet-18 was 0.1% higher than AlexNet and 0.6% higher than the custom ConvNet. The custom ConvNet achieved an accuracy of more than 90%. Transfer learning models achieved an average accuracy of nearly 97%.

architecture_used.main_model: Three models: 1) Custom 7-layered Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained from scratch; 2) Modified AlexNet (transfer learning); 3) Modified ResNet-18 (transfer learning)
architecture_used.pretraining: AlexNet and ResNet-18 were pre-trained on the ImageNet database. The custom CNN was trained from scratch.

custom_cnn.convolutional_blocks: 4 blocks. Filters increased from 8 to 16, 32, 64, and 128 in successive layers. Each block had a 3x3 convolutional filter, batch normalization, ReLU activation, and 2x2 MaxPooling.
custom_cnn.fully_connected_layers: First FC layer: 256 neurons. Second FC layer: 2 neurons (output).
custom_cnn.activation_functions: ReLU for convolutional layers, SoftMax for final classification layer.
custom_cnn.regularization: Batch normalization after each convolutional layer. Dropout with value 0.3 applied before the final layer.

modified_alexnet.modifications: Last dense layer (1000 neurons) replaced with 2 neurons. New fully connected layer with 256 neurons added, followed by ReLU activation.

modified_resnet.modifications: Last dense layer (1000 neurons) replaced with 2 neurons. Last four convolutional layers and the last fully connected layer were unfrozen and trained.
modified_resnet.parameters: Not explicitly reported for modified version

custom_cnn.learning_rate: Initial 0.0001, reduced by dividing by the number of epochs after every epoch.

modified_resnet.epochs: Not explicitly reported (kept same as other experiments)

modified_alexnet.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (kept same as other experiments)
modified_alexnet.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (kept same as other experiments)
modified_alexnet.learning_rate: Not explicitly reported (kept same as other experiments)
modified_alexnet.batch_size: Not explicitly reported (kept same as other experiments)
modified_alexnet.epochs: Not explicitly reported (kept same as other experiments)

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation (90°, 180°, 270°), flipping (upside down, left to right). Generated 6 samples per original image.
training_details.hardware: Google Colaboratory with 12GB NVIDIA Tesla K80 GPU and 25GB RAM

classification_or_output_head.custom_cnn: 2-neuron fully connected layer followed by SoftMax activation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Custom seven-layered deep convolutional neural network (CNN)
preprocessing_techniques: Artifact removal: Cropping and image processing techniques were used to remove dermoscopic artifacts such as dark corner artifact frames, ruler markings, and hairs.
ROI Cropping: The center region of interest (ROI) of the skin lesion was cropped from the high-resolution original images (2560 × 1920) to preserve lesion shape and features.
Resizing: Images were resized to 224 × 224 × 3 to fit the CNN input size after ROI cropping.
Data Augmentation: Rotation (90°, 180°, 270°) and flipping (upside down, left to right) were applied to generate 6 samples per original image.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated to angles of 90°, 180°, and 270°.
Images were flipped upside down and from left to right.
The combination of rotation and flipping generated six samples from a single original training sample, resulting in a six-fold increase in data size.

conclusions: The proposed custom seven-layered deep convolutional neural network (CNN) trained from scratch achieved an accuracy of more than 90% for classifying acral melanoma and benign nevi.
Transfer learning approaches using modified AlexNet and ResNet-18 achieved an average accuracy of nearly 97%, which is comparable to state-of-the-art methods.
The modified ResNet-18 model demonstrated the best performance among the tested models, with an ROC curve value 0.1% higher than AlexNet and 0.6% higher than the custom ConvNet.
The study confirms that deep learning systems can effectively classify skin cancer and assist dermatologists in the clinical decision-making process for the early diagnosis of acral melanoma.
Data augmentation and transfer learning were effective strategies to address the limited availability of large datasets for rare melanoma subtypes like acral melanoma.

The custom-built ConvNet produced promising results comparable to transfer learning despite being trained from scratch, highlighting the potential of effective hyperparameter tuning for rare disease classification.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small dataset size (724 images) and lack of external validation, as all data were sourced from a single institution (Yonsei University Health System, South Korea), raising concerns about generalizability to other populations.
The dataset is imbalanced with 350 Acral Melanoma cases and 374 Benign Nevus cases, which may bias model performance.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean ± standard deviation) and ROC curves.

The paper acknowledges that Acral Melanoma is rare and lacks large public datasets, necessitating data augmentation and transfer learning, which limits the direct applicability of the custom CNN trained from scratch to larger, more diverse datasets.
Data leakage risks are present due to the use of a single-center dataset without independent external validation cohorts, potentially overestimating clinical utility.

The custom 7-layered CNN achieved >90% accuracy, but transfer learning models (ResNet-18, AlexNet) performed better (~97%), suggesting that pre-trained features are critical for this task given the limited data.

The study does not report sensitivity and specificity separately for the custom CNN, only providing overall accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC, making direct comparison with other diagnostic metrics difficult.

The preprocessing steps (artifact removal, ROI cropping) were applied to all images, but the impact of these steps on feature preservation and potential information loss is not quantitatively assessed.
